summary: Steve Yegge returns to blogging after several years to announce his mobile MMORPG game Wyvern, which he's been developing for 20 years (originally started in 1996). He shares an amusing anecdote about his wife's pet monkey stealing her in-game loot, demonstrating how he used Jython's interactive evaluation to debug and fix the problem at runtime. The post compares iOS and Android development, strongly favoring iOS for its intuitive APIs and better monetization potential. He details his frustrating experience with Apple's App Store review process, including being rejected for IPv6 compliance issues and being forced to implement native account authentication. Despite the challenges, he remains optimistic about the game's future given his loyal player base.
key_points: Building a real MMORPG is an enormous undertaking - Yegge estimates hundreds of person-years of total effort, with significant contributions from volunteer area designers
iOS development feels familiar and intuitive compared to Android, which he describes as 'far, far, FAR from what you would expect' from any other UI framework
iOS monetization is 4-10x better than Android because Android has cultivated a 'frugal audience' through marketing and hardware choices
Apple's App Store review process is long and arbitrary - they can force major feature requests on developers with little recourse
Modern development infrastructure (GitHub, cloud platforms, Stack Overflow, contractor services) has dramatically increased productivity compared to the late 1990s
money_quotes: I'm pretty sure that in most states, accidentally replacing your wife's hard-earned treasure with bananas and bits of fur is legal grounds for divorce.
Normally you get a monster's inventory the old-fashioned way. You pay the iron price.
I always gauge how edgy my blog posts are by how likely I am to get fired over them, and my little indicator is redlining.
Twitter's philosophy is, roughly: 'Go fuck yourself.'
You're talking Java 1.0.2, with that O'Reilly book with the referees on the front. You had to roll everything yourself back then. Uphill both ways, in the snow.
But really, anyone can do it. You just gotta want it bad enough.
All I'm doing is copying code from Stack Overflow, really I have no shame whatsoever, and Xcode works great for that.
key_insight: Building something meaningful requires enormous persistence over years or decades, but modern development infrastructure has made ambitious solo projects more achievable than ever before.
